entry.title: A.I Assisted Transition at your own Pace: BW7.5 to BDC Pilot Experiences
entry.content:
This blog is part of a series related to a complex BW7.5 customer case where we explored together the opportunity to transition to BDC. You can find the intro blog below, plus the links to the subsequent blogs will be listed here -

  •  Link 1
  • Link 2 
  • Link 3

The Time is Now

At the time of typing this it is still early 2026. The year’s priorities and goals are being set. For many of our BW customers, the end of maintenance deadline set at the end of 2027 for NW 7.5 is looming large. Questions such as the below are being asked –

  • How can we maintain what we have built over many years without rebuilding from the ground up?
  • How can we make use of the latest and greatest from A.I?
  • Can we continue to use our existing skills?
  • Can we remain compliant to our regulatory requirements?

The goal of this series of blogs is to outline what customers on BW 7.5 can do to modernise their mission critical system of engagement at their own pace whilst embracing A.I, ensuring they avoid expensive regret cost migrations to third party platforms that can often spiral, seldom delivering on the initial promise. These blogs are based on first hand experiences in a pilot project for BW7.5 to BDC.

Interested to learn more? Read on

The Problem Statement

BW 7.5 is the last version of the ABAP stack SAP Business Warehouse that was initially released in the late 1990’s, it delivers out of the box capabilities (business content, extractor logic, authorisation concepts etc) to manage complex third normal form data structures from SAP ECC (legacy SAP application around finance, HR, supply chain etc) across a layered scalable architecture (LSA). In 2016, the ‘new’ BW was released called BW/4HANA with a roadmap to 2040+, triggering a timeline that will culminate in 2027 with the end of mainstream maintenace of the ABAP version BW7.5.

However, many customers for a variety of reasons chose not to move to BW4. Either they have too much logic built in objects not easily ported (for example APDs, BEx) or they were enticed by promises made by best of breed data vendors in cloud native data lake architectures to migrate their BW workload. Often these project spiral in costs and effort without delivering sufficient value.

The Solution

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), released in February 2025 is SAP’s fully managed SaaS solution built for modern workloads and to natively handle data-as-a-service from across the SAP Business Suite applications (S/4HANA Public and Private, SuccessFactors, Concur etc). This is achieved at the click of a button whereby ‘Data Products’ are loaded and kept in sync with backend transactional systems, removing the leg work for customers looking to get rapid value from their data. Business Content and Intelligent applications are installed via the BDC Cockpit, surfacing KPIs for decision making across a host of business areas, with a vast amount more to come in 2026. With BDC, SAP Databricks, SAP Snowflake and other best of breed solutions work in tandem with SAP Datasphere on the semantically rich data products providing customers a unified data architecture to deliver next generation A.I use cases.

What adds to the excitement of BW customers setting their new year goals? BDC is the go-to solution for SAP Business Warehouse. BDC provides a strong roadmap for customers to modernise their BW7.5 environment at their own pace, retaining their investments, their skills, keeping their data available and opening their rich sets of historic business data for A.I. This is achieved through the ‘Lift, Shift & Innovate’ approach that allows for large complex BWs to move to a managed cloud service (Private Cloud Edition) of BW within the formation of SAP Business Data Cloud.

The Lift phase moves existing SAP BW systems as-is to SAP-managed cloud infrastructure, extending maintenance timelines (2030 instead of 2027) and reducing operational overhead. The Shift phase exposes SAP BW data as Data Products using the Data Product Generator tool, enabling integration with SAP Datasphere and external data sources while preserving existing use cases. The Innovate phase explores SAP-managed Data Products and Intelligent Applications to replace legacy SAP BW scenarios with modern analytics, AI capabilities, and access to an open partner ecosystem. This phased approach mitigates risk by maintaining existing investments while providing a clear path to cloud-native analytics and reduced Total Cost of Ownership.

There are some prerequisites for your BW system to fulfil prior to embarking on the journey with BDC, the minimum version supporting the data product generator is SPS 24 and it needs to be running on HANA.

Many large global organisations reliant on BW for their strategic reporting, planning/business steering have already begun the transition to BDC to realise the benefits of modernisation to cloud native at their own pace.

‘Vibe Migration’

In Autumn/Winter 2025 the SAP EMEA and Global Solution Advisory teams worked cohesively on a pilot to demonstrate the BW to BDC move for a specific customer case.

This case assessed all aspects of a complex BW7.5 system after the Lift to PCE, demonstrating how the Shift and Innovate phases can look using the modern tools available in SAP BDC with a focus on SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud and Artificial Intelligence. Throughout the subsequent blog posts we will share the findings with specifics on how end of life (EoL) objects such as APDs, BEx and other scenarios can be covered either through automated migration to SAP BDC Datasphere, A.I assisted conversion or rebuild.

Please stay tuned for the next in this series of blog posts related to the Data Product Generator.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Planning with Live Snowflake Data
entry.content:
Introduction

In Q4 2025, we released live versions for planning models in SAP Analytics Cloud: 

  • In seamless planning, you can connect live to fact data in SAP Datasphere (blogpost)
  • In non-seamless planning scenarios, you can connect to the live SQL sources available in SAP Analytics Cloud. Up until now, Google BigQuery was the only available source (blogpost)

With QRC1 2026, Snowflake is added to the portfolio of live sources and with that, you can now reference Snowflake data for planning without replication!

 

How does it work?

In my blogpost on live versions from Google BigQuery, I demonstrated how to add live data to an existing model. So this time, we will take another approach and create a planning model from data.

  • Go to the modeler
  • Create model
  • Create model from data
  • Choose Snowflake. You can filter the list of connections to Data Storage “Cloud”
  • Choose a connection.
  • Choose a view
  • The model is created along the source view’s structures. You see the instant live connection in the data preview

Model creation from live data

You can now work with this model as you know from model’s without live versions. In the example below, we remove dimensions and measures that we do not need. We re-create one of the measures as a live calculation.

Calculations on live data

We can also leverage the data in data actions. See this example where we copy the Actual data into a plan version with a factor. The actual version that you see is sourced directly from Snowflake. When we copy the data into a plan version, the plan data is stored in the fact table of our model in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

Planning

 

FAQ

Where can I use this feature?

This feature is available for SAP Analytics Cloud models that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere (=not using seamless planning).

Can I use any source view?

Yes. However, not all data types are supported (details).

What can I do with live versions? (And what can I not do?)

  • You can display the live version data in the model data foundation and in tables, charts, etc. in stories.
  • You can reference the live version data in model and story calculations as well as data actions (incl. advanced formulas).
  • You cannot write back to the source. You can copy the live version data into planning versions via copy/paste in the table, data actions and version management.

How do I see if data is live or not?

  • In models, a live version is highlighted in the data preview. In the version dimension, you see the data source per version. 
  • In tables, live version data is locked for data entry and formatted accordingly. Reasons for unplannable data give accurate information.

 

Conclusion

Live data access in planning models represents a massive opportunity. SAP Analytics Cloud aligns with SAP Business Data Cloud’s ambition to be open and keeps on expanding its live connectivity portfolio. Look forward to use your Azure data for planning in the future and follow the respective innovation in the Roadmap Explorer.

entry.title: What's New on SAP Analytics Cloud Home Screen 2026 Q1 QRC: New Admin Cards
entry.content:
I'm excited to introduce three new Administrator Cards on the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Home screen in the Q1 QRC release. These cards give administrators a direct, customizable presence on users’ home screens and surface the operational insights needed to keep your SAC environment healthy and well governed with less time spent hunting for root causes.

What’s new

1 - Admin custom card

  • What it is: A fully customizable card that lets administrators display access links, announcements, and any other custom messages directly on users’ home screens.
  • How to create it: from the admin settings and under default appearance tab, click "+ Add Custom Card"

  •  What you can include: images, text, tags, and links tailored to the message or audience.

  • Important behavior: If an administrator locks and saves the custom card, users will not be able to move or hide it from their home screen.
  • Benefits: Deliver timely announcements (upgrades, maintenance windows, training links), promote key content or processes, and ensure critical guidance is seen by target audiences.

2- Administrative actions card

  • What it is: A focused card that summarizes critical recommendations for admins.
  • Purpose: Reduce time spent on root-cause analysis by surfacing key actionable items and prioritized recommendations.
  • Benefits: Enables faster, proactive housekeeping and maintenance helping admins resolve issues earlier and keep the system running smoothly.

 3 - System monitor card

  • What it is: A dedicated admin card that brings a concise, comprehensive summary of system health to the Home screen.
  • What it shows: critical system metrics, user/license health, performance hotspots, and governance-related recommendations.
  • Benefits: Empowers administrators to spot trends, identify performance issues quickly, and make informed decisions about scaling, licensing, or governance actions.

 How these cards help your organization

  • Improve visibility: Place critical notices and system health indicators where admins and users will see them daily.
  • Save time: The Administrative Actions card reduces investigation time by collecting high-priority recommendations in one place.
  • Encourage consistency: Locked custom cards ensure essential guidance (policies, access instructions, or emergency contacts) remains visible.
  • Drive governance: The System Monitor card makes it easier to track license usage, performance patterns, and governance recommendations without running separate reports.

Quick admin tips

  • Use images and tags on the custom card to increase clarity and engagement for announcements and links.
  • Lock and save a custom card only when you want fixed visibility for all users; otherwise let users personalize their Home experience.
  • Treat the Administrative Actions card as your operational to-do list, prioritize items and turn recurring recommendations into scheduled maintenance tasks.
  • Monitor the System Monitor card regularly to catch performance hotspots early and adjust configurations or scale resources as needed.

Getting started

These cards arrive with the Q1 QRC release. If you’re an admin, check your Home screen configuration options to enable and customize these cards. If you need to align messaging across teams, plan your card content and locking strategy before publishing.

entry.title: SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM - Creating a connection with SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise [SAC]
entry.content:
SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 (V2) and Enterprise Service Management (ESM) deliver modern, modular customer‑experience capabilities. But as organizations scale, the real differentiator becomes analytics—the ability to unify sales, service, and enterprise operations into a single insights layer.

SAP provides two major analytics options:

  1. Embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (eSAC) – included and built into V2 and ESM
  2. SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise Edition – the full standalone analytics platform

Pre-requisities

  • Create SAP Technical Support ticket Component to choose (CEC-CRM-ANA) to enable Enterprise live connectivity
  • SSO must be enabled in both systems (SAP Sales & Service Cloud v2 & SAP Analytics Cloud)
  • Login via SSO in Sales and Service Cloud v2 Application.
  • Login to Enterprise SAC using the same SSO as used above.
  • Now try to create the connection and it should be successful (shown in below steps - screenshots).

Before you proceed to Steps : Note : While doing handshake - Login to both system should be in same browser but in different tabs.

Step by Step via Video :  Setting up the Connection

Step 1 : SSO must be enabled in order to work both Applications ( SSC v2 & SAC)

Step 2 : Login to SAP Analytics Cloud (Enterprise License version) and you must be Administrator to config

Step 3 : Select Connect to Live Data - Select a Data Source - SAP Cloud Application

Step 4 : Select from dropdown SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2

Step 5 : Enter the Application URL of your SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 URL then immediately the Redirection happens to authenticate against both systems if the same IDP matches with successfull response.

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM with SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise unlocks powerful, cross-functional insights. While eSAC provides rapid, embedded visualization, SAC Enterprise enables enterprise-scale reporting and combines data from multiple systems.

For organizations seeking lighter setups, custom integration patterns using embedded Analytics within v2, using V2 REST APIs and BTP or SAP Databricks are viable alternatives.

By following the steps and architecture options outlined in this guide, your organization can create a unified analytics environment that empowers sales, service, and enterprise teams with real-time, actionable insights.

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: Certified academy for data analyst - SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Certification journeys can feel overwhelming.  Content everywhere, limited time, real project pressure.

That’s exactly why we created the Partner learning accelerator: Certified academy for data analyst – SAP Analytics Cloud.

This 6-hour live academy is designed for partners who want to prepare for the SAP Certified – Data Analyst – SAP Analytics Cloud certification with confidence.  Not just theory, but reinforced core concepts, hands-on practice, and exam-relevant topics you’ll actually use in customer projects.

We focus on data modeling, analysis, visualization, and planning—plus how these capabilities come together in real project scenarios.  The thing is, certification readiness isn’t just about passing an exam.  It’s about feeling ready when a customer asks the hard questions.

If you already understand analytics basics and have some exposure to SAP Analytics Cloud, this academy will help you connect the dots and sharpen your skills.

👉 Register now and invest six focused hours in your growth as an SAP partner.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00009814.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPAnalyticsCloud #SAPLearning #DataAnalytics

entry.title: CFOs seeking AI – Optimal decisions using SAP IBP in Product Making (manufacturing)
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION - THE PROBLEM

Today, every business is seeking efficiency and productivity improvement with Artificial Intelligence. This means that drawing upon available data in systems, faster decisions can be made with Artificial Intelligence. However, most teams in FIN (Finance) are used to doing their analysis in spreadsheets offline, which works when decisions come to Finance for approval. However, Artificial Intelligence provides a shortcut - more decisions can be made away from the centre - decentralised - drawing on the available data sets in current systems. 

This article takes my earlier Article on  How Finance & Supply Chain can get closer together to a  new level, and brings more into focus my last Article on “Mind the Gap” – Improves ROI, Cost & Margin by Merging Planning Processes. 

UNDERSTANDING THE CONTEXT

Operations in Product Making (manufacturing) is perhaps the most decentralised location where decisions are being made at lightening speed - decisions that impact every area of the business: revenue, customer service, profit and working capital. However, these decisions are more often made on the basis of operational KPIs and not what is best for the business financially, because Operations don't have access to the financial implications of decisions until after the decisoin is made.

CFOs and FIN (Finance) Teams remotely setting Targets, KPIs and Performance Management is no longer enough when using Artificial Intelligence - its too slow! 

Operations in Product Making (manufacturing) have historically looked to Finance to validate financial outcomes of operational decisions. However, the advances taking place in Artificial Intelligence  mean that Operations can leverage Artificial Intelligence for rapid decision-making support, leaving Finance completely out of the short-term decision-making loop - unless - Finance provide the available FIN (Finance) data, currently held in spreadsheets, to the operational planning systems so that the  FIN (Finance) data can be used by Artificial Intelligence - especially the forward looking FIN (Finance) forecast data that is needed to guide forward Operational decision-making with Artificial Intelligence and to deliver the optimal financial decisions in Operations. 

SOLVING THE EMERGENT PROBLEM 

There are many ways financial data can be embedded in the system for use by Artificial Intelligence. My focus in this article is to talk to the rationale of using SAP Integrated Business Planning for Product Making (manufacturing) businesses.   

Why ?

SAP Integrated Business Planning is a collaborative decision-making platform that brings together in one space all the available past, present and future information required for decision making in Operations for Product Making (manufacturing) businesses. Operations use the available past, present and future information in systems to make operational decisions and trade-offs, today, that are felt by the business financially for the months that follow.  Artificial Intelligence provides a more efficient basis for making these decisions faster, based on the data available. 

What needs to change ?

FIN (Finance)data in systems is typically retrospective, or at best set up for transactional use in the present. Operations are making decisions today that impact the future, and forward looking FIN (Finance) data is most often held off-line in spreadsheets, enabling financial analysts to constantly iterate and update their analysis independently of the core business systems. This means Operations are making a myriad of daily decisions using FIN (Finance) information that is not the latest available. Depending on how out-of-date the FIN (Finance) data is in the business system, future decision-making will be financially sub-optimal and Artificial Intelligence will accelerate sub-optimal financial decision-making in Operations, today, with the impact of sub-optimal financial performance of the business as a whole, in the months that follow.  

Outside of SAP Integrated Business Planning, there are few options in the market that can boast native integration with underlying ERP systems - standard integration connections to both SAP ECC and S/4HANA - to bring past, present and future data together in one data space, and the ability to use embedded Artificial Intelligence without the complexity and cost of AI Agents, or Agentic AI - where integrations with Agentic AI need to be thoroughly tested prior to use.

THE SOLUTION FORWARD

Operations can make ALL decisions in SAP Integrated Business Planning. 

The accelerated adoption of Artificial Intelligence means faster decisions using the data transferred between ERP and Operations Planning. Using SAP Integrated Business Planning enables all the FIN (Finance) data that Finance want considered in the future using the rapid decision making of Artificial Intelligence can be made available to Operations and the optimal FIN (Finance) decision can be made - fast!

TODAY'S NEW CHALLENGES 

In short, Artificial Intelligence accelerates the need :

  • to implement SAP Integrated Business Planning in Product Making (manufacturing) businesses.
  • for FIN (Finance) to share FIN (Finance) Data across the business to assure the optimal business financial outcome.
  • for FIN (Finance) to lead implementation of new technologies across the business, prior to Artificial Intelligence adoption, to assure the optimal business financial outcome

entry.title: Integrate planning and risk management with SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Planning without risk is just optimism with formatting.

That’s why we’re bringing you “Integrate planning and risk management with SAP Analytics Cloud”.  A live session built for partners who want sharper decisions — fast.

With SAP Analytics Cloud Compass, planning stops being a one-number forecast and becomes a range of real, probability-based outcomes.  You’ll see how Monte Carlo simulations expose uncertainty, reduce manual scenario work, and give teams a shared language for decision-making.

No heavy math.
No black boxes.
Just intuitive simulations that business users can actually trust.

We’ll cover what risk-enhanced planning really means, show Compass in action, and explain why planning and risk were always meant to work together (long-lost twins, reunited).

If you’re focused on xP&A and smarter planning conversations with customers, don’t miss this.

👉 Register now and join us live.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012965.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPAnalyticsCloud #SAPPartners #xPA #PlanningInnovation

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 動的テキスト
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、フィルタ条件や選択内容をテキストとして画面上に明示したい際に使用いただける動的テキストの作成についてご紹介いたします。

 

目次：

  • SAP Analytics Cloudにおける動的テキストの概要
  • 動的テキストの作成方法[ハンズオン]

※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成⼿順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。
※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画⾯キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年2⽉に作成しています。）

 

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける動的テキストの概要

動的テキストはSACのストーリー内に表示する文章/チャート/テーブルなどのタイトルに、フィルタや入力コントロール、モデル変数などの条件を自動的に反映させる仕組みです。ユーザーが地域や期間などの条件を切り替えると、それに応じてテキストの内容も変わり、表示しているデータの文脈を明確に伝えられます。

例えば「現在〇〇地域の売上を表示しています」や「対象期間：2024年第3四半期」のように、分析対象を明示することで、グラフや数値だけでは分かりにくい条件を補足できます。

PDFとしてエクスポートした場合も選択中の条件が反映されたテキストが残るため、資料としての説明力も向上します。

詳しくは SAP Help Portal も参照ください：
チャートのタイトルに対する動的テキストの追加 | SAP Help Portal
テーブルのタイトルに対する動的テキストの追加 | SAP Help Portal
ストーリーへの動的テキストの追加 | SAP Help Portal

 

動的テキストの作成方法

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成で作成したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

※はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - フィルタの作成 - SAP Communityを事前に実施されている方は0.は不要です。作成されたストーリーを複製して1.のハンズオンを開始してください。

0.1. ナビゲーションバーのファイルより作成したストーリーを開き、「編集」をクリックします。

 

0.2. 「Page_1」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「複製」をクリックしてページを複製します。

 

0.3. 「Page_1 (1)」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「タイトルの編集」をクリックし、タイトルを「動的テキスト」に変更します。

 

0.4. 表示のメニューから「左側のパネル」をクリックし、アセットを表示させます。

　0.5. 「入力コントロール」をドラッグアンドドロップでストーリーの任意の場所に配置します。

 

0.6. 「ディメンション」をクリックし、「事業セグメント」、「メンバーによるフィルタ」をクリックします。

 

 

0.7. 「すべてのメンバー」にチェックを入れ、「OK」をクリックします。

フィルタの作成についてはこちらをご覧ください：はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - フィルタの作成 - SAP Community

 

1. 動的テキストを作成しよう！

1.1. 数値チャートのタイトルに動的テキストを適用します。「売上高」と表示されているテキストを選択し、マウスを右クリックします。

 

1.2.「＋追加」から、「動的テキスト」をクリックします。

 

 

1.3. 動的テキストの挿入のメニューが出るので、「入力コントロール」、「事業セグメント」を選択し、「作成」をクリックします。
「○○の売上高」となるように、「売上高」の前に「の」を追加すると見やすくなります。

 

1.4. 動的テキストを作成することができました！
入力コントロールでベストITSを選択すると、テキストも連動してベストITSになっていることが分かります。

 

　1.5. 左上の「保存」をクリックして、ストーリーを保存します。

 

今回は動的テキストのご紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Beyond the Workhorse: What I’ve Learned Modernizing U.S. Utilities
entry.content:
Introduction

In my decade-plus of architecting for major U.S. utilities—from the high-pressure power corridors of the Northeast to the expanding grids in the South—I’ve seen a shift in what "success" looks like. For a long time, the goal was simple: Keep the lights on. But as we hit 2026, that benchmark is failing. Aggressive decarbonization and the wave of decentralized energy (DERs) like rooftop solar and EV charging are putting a massive strain on our trusted workhorse, SAP IS-U.

1.     The Reality Check: When the "Standard" Hits the Ceiling

IS-U is a dependable system for meter-to-cash, but it was designed for a world of predictable, one-way power flow. In my recent field work, I've kept hitting three "Legacy Burdens":

  • The Batch Job Bottleneck: Waiting for overnight runs to understand grid load is a non-starter in a smart-grid world.
  • The "Spaghetti" Problem: Years of custom ABAP "Z-code" have made some environments so brittle that a simple integration takes months of testing.
  • The 2027 Clock: S/4HANA migration isn't just an upgrade; it’s a race to preserve business logic buried in 20 years of customizations.

 

2.     My Architecture Philosophy: The "Clean Core" Mindset

The biggest shift I’ve driven in recent programs is moving away from the instinct to "customize inside the ERP." By adopting a Clean Core strategy, we treat SAP BTP as our innovative layer.

This keeps the S/4HANA core stable. While the core handles the heavy transactions, the "brain" (SAP BTP) manages the fast-changing logic. This gives us speed without risking the stability of the billing engine.

3.     Practical Field Notes: Real Impact with BTP

This isn't theoretical. I’ve seen this move the needle in North American programs, including work with one of the largest utility companies in north America:

  • Integration Suite: This is the "glue" connecting AMI, weather data, and regulatory feeds without brittle, point-to-point interfaces.
  • Hindsight to Foresight: We are now using SAP Datasphere to identify transformer stress before it leads to failure.
  • The Human Element: Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) rises when we use BTP to send proactive usage alerts mid-month.

4.     A Deep Dive: The Automated Reconnection Case

I’m particularly proud of rebuilding the customer reconnection process. Traditionally, this was a multi-day headache. We turned it into a real-time event.

How we built it:

  1. Payment Trigger: Customer pays via an app (SAP Build).
  2. Instant Clearing: SAP Integration Suite pushes the update to FI-CA.
  3. Decision & Execution: We applied a 3-layered governance protocol to ensure only "Safe-to-Reconnect" signals hit the AMI smart meters.

4.     Lessons from the Trenches: What Leaders Need to Know

This isn't just about clicking buttons in the BTP cockpit; it's a cultural shift. If you are leading a team, remember:

  • Close the Skill Gap: Veteran ABAP teams have the domain knowledge—help them translate that into Node.js and CAP.
  • Avoid "App Sprawl": Without governance, you’ll end up with a hundred disconnected micro-apps.
  • Data Residency: For U.S. utilities, compliance for billing data must be built into the architecture from Day 1.

Conclusion: The Future is Composed

Across the programs I’ve led, the winning pattern is clear: S/4HANA is the Heart, BTP is the Brain, and IoT/Integration is the Senses.

SAP IS-U has served us well, but the energy transition demands more agility. The question isn't if we should move to BTP—it's how quickly we can use it to stop being reactive and start being proactive.

I'd love to hear from other architects: What's the messiest "Z-code" logic you're currently trying to move out of your core? Let's discuss in the comments.

entry.title: Story Versioning in SAP Analytics Cloud: Design with Confidence & Iterate Without Fear!
entry.content:
Designing impactful stories in SAP Analytics Cloud is rarely a straight line. Instead, Story Designers iterate constantly by experimenting with layouts, refining narratives, adjusting calculations, and incorporating stakeholder feedback. Until now, that iterative process often came with a trade-off: move fast, or play it safe.

Without a native way to manage major story iterations, designers relied on manual copies, naming conventions like “Corporate_Overview_Final_v7_REAL_FINAL,” or external documentation to track changes. This made it harder to experiment confidently, collaborate transparently, and recover from changes that didn’t land as expected.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC, we’re introducing Story Versioning. A foundational capability that gives Story Designers the freedom to iterate, experiment, and refine their work, while staying fully in control.

Story Versioning

Story Versioning is designed to be simple by default, while still supporting real-world design workflows. There’s no separate tool or workflow to learn as it is embedded directly into the existing authoring experience, allowing designers to adopt it naturally without disrupting how they already work. Let's look into a few of the core capabilities. 

Explicit Major Versions with Designer Control

Story Designers can create up to 10 versions per story, each acting as a major version. Creating a new version is an intentional action and are never created automatically in the background.

Once the maximum of ten versions is reached, designers choose which existing version to delete before creating a new one. This approach ensures flexibility, allowing designers to retain the versions that matter most rather than assuming the oldest version is no longer relevant.

Each version can also have a descriptive note, making it easy to capture context such as review milestones, experiments, or key design changes.

View, Restore, or Save Versions Safely

Any previous version can be opened in a read-only view, allowing designers to review earlier iterations without impacting the current version.

From there, designers can either save the version as a new story or restore it. When a version is restored, SAP Analytics Cloud automatically creates a backup of the current version, ensuring that all changes remain reversible and no work is lost unintentionally.

Always Aligned with the Latest Dependent Objects

Stories often consume multiple dependent objects such as models, themes, composites, and so on, each with its own lifecycle. Story Versioning always consumes the latest state of these dependent objects.

Predictable Transport Behavior

When transporting a story using the Content Network (ACN), only the current active version is included. This keeps transports clean and predictable, while aligning with existing lifecycle and promotion workflows.

Version Visibility in the File Repository

All created versions are visible through the file repository, giving designers transparency into a story’s evolution directly from content management views.

 

Why Story Versioning Matters

Story Versioning fundamentally changes how Story Designers work in SAP Analytics Cloud.

It enables confident experimentation without fear of breaking a story, clearer tracking across major design milestones, safer collaboration when incorporating feedback, and simpler recovery from changes that don’t pan out.

Most importantly, it shifts story design from a cautious, copy-heavy workflow to one that encourages creative exploration with guardrails. This capability also lays the foundation for future versioning for other objects in SAP Analytics Cloud, allowing all personas to focus less on managing risk and more on completing their tasks.

entry.title: Expanding Visual Analysis: New Area Chart, Pareto Chart, and Stacked Area Enhancements
entry.content:
As analytics adoption grows, Story Designers are increasingly expected to move beyond static reporting and deliver visual analysis that explains trends, highlights drivers, and supports prioritization.

While SAP Analytics Cloud already offers a strong foundation of visualizations, designers often faced limitations when trying to identify key contributors behind a metric, show how values evolve over time without visual clutter, or clearly communicate proportional impact and cumulative effects.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC, SAP Analytics Cloud expands its out-of-the-box visualization capabilities with new and enhanced charts: Pareto Chart, Area Chart, and improvements to the Stacked Area!

Pareto Chart: Focus on What Truly Drives Impact

The Pareto Chart is designed to help Story Designers to prioritize focus, explain trade-offs, and guide action instead of treating all contributors as equally important.

We wanted the creation of a Pareto Chart to be intentionally simple. Story Designers just have to bind a primary structure (Measure or Account depending on the Model Type) and select the dimension across which the analysis should run. The Pareto Visualization is then automatically generated, sorted from highest to lowest, including the cumulative percentage which increases to 100%. 

Furthermore, we allow additional Primary Structures as supplementary columns for context without impacting the Cumulative Percentage Calculation. It provides Story Designers the option to enrich the analysis while keeping the pareto logic clean and mathematically correct.

⚠️ For the Cumulative Percentage, Pareto Charts rely on a Running Total Calculation and therefore is not supported for data models that do not allow for the creation of a Running Total Calculation in Stories, such as SAP BW and SAP IBP. 

Area Chart: A Clear Alternative to Stacked Visuals

The new Area Chart introduces a powerful option for visualizing trends over time while maintaining focus on the overall shape and direction of the data. Compared to Stacked Area Charts, Area Charts emphasize trend and magnitude rather than composition, reduce visual complexity when multiple series are present, and make it easier to follow a single measure’s evolution across time. It makes Area Charts especially valuable when the analytical goal is trend interpretation rather than part-to-whole comparison.

Stacked Area Chart Enhancements: More Expressive, More Flexible

The Stacked Area Chart has been enhanced to support use cases that matter to you the most! These include the introduction of a 100% Stacked Area Chart, enabling designers to clearly visualize proportional contribution over time. We've also added the configuration to adjust the line to a smooth line, which also applies to the Area Chart and offers consistency with our other Chart Types. 

Why This Matters for Story Designers

These visualization enhancements significantly expand what Story Designers can do out of the box in SAP Analytics Cloud rather than relying on Custom Widgets.

As SAP Analytics Cloud continues to evolve its visualization portfolio, designers can rely less on workarounds and more on purpose-built visuals that match analytical intent.

ⓘ Keep an eye out on the roadmap explorer, as new Chart Types such as the Gauge, Funnel, and Sankey will be coming soon!

entry.title: From Spreadsheets to a Financial Digital Twin: Reinventing FP&A with SAP
entry.content:
What Exactly Is a Financial Digital Twin?

At its core, a financial digital twin is a virtual model of your financial reality that is:

  • Connected: Continuously fed by actuals and operational data.
  • Intelligent: Built on business drivers and assumptions, not just static accounts.
  • Interactive: Able to simulate scenarios and show impacts on P&L, balance sheet, and cash in near real time.

You can think of it as a flight simulator for your finance function: instead of testing strategies in the real world (with real risk), you run them through the twin and see how they behave under different conditions.

Why CFOs and FP&A Leaders Should Care

  1. From Static Budgets to Continuous Planning

Traditional FP&A runs on an annual budget and a handful of reforecasts. A digital twin enables:

  • Rolling forecasts that update when new data arrives, not just at quarter-end.
  • Near real-time visibility into how operational changes (orders, production, collections) flow into revenue, margins, and cash.

The result is a finance function that reacts to shocks (demand drops, price spikes, supply disruptions) in days, not months.

2. Better Decisions Through Scenario-Based Thinking

A financial digital twin is built for “what if” questions:

  • What if volume drops by 10% in our top three markets?
  • What if we move 15% of sales to a lower-margin channel?
  • What if FX moves against us by 5% for two quarters?

Instead of debating assumptions in PowerPoint, you run scenarios in the twin and see quantified impacts on earnings, leverage, and liquidity. This supports:

  • More confident guidance to the board and investors.
  • Better capital allocation and risk-management decision

 

3. A Shared View Across Finance and Operations

In many organizations, operations and finance look at different numbers and speak different languages. The digital twin creates a shared environment where:

  • Operations sees how their KPIs (throughput, yield, service levels) hit EBITDA and cash.
  • Finance sees the operational levers behind variances.

This alignment turns FP&A into a collaborative business partner rather than a reporting factory.

External Data ( machine info, Forex data etc)

The SAP Building Blocks of a Financial Digital Twin

If your core runs on SAP, you already have most of the building blocks. The opportunity is to deliberately assemble them into a coherent digital twin architecture.

  1. SAP S/4HANA (and Central Finance): The Single Source of Truth

The Universal Journal in S/4HANA gives you a single, granular source of financial and controlling data. This is the transactional backbone of your twin:

  • All postings end up in one place (ACDOCA) with rich dimensions (company code, profit center, segment, customer, product, etc.).
  • You can drill from consolidated views down to individual line items without leaving the system.

If you run multiple ERPs, Central Finance can pull these into a harmonized S/4HANA-based finance hub. The digital twin then represents the full group, not just one system.

2. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC): Planning, Forecasting, and Decision Cockpit

SAC is where much of the FP&A “front end” of the twin lives:

  • Planning models aligned to S/4HANA dimensions and hierarchies.
  • Driver-based budgeting where users adjust volumes, prices, and headcount instead of typing values into every account.
  • Scenario management with versions for base, upside, downside, and stress-test cases.
  • Dashboards and stories to visualize actuals, plan, and forecast side by side.

With live connectivity to S/4HANA, SAC can show up-to-date actuals while keeping planning data in the same space, eliminating reconciliation headaches.

3. SAP PaPM: The Economic Engine

A true digital twin needs to reflect how value is actually created and consumed, not just how it is reported. SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM) helps by:

  • Modeling complex cost and revenue allocation logic (cost-to-serve, shared services, transfer pricing).
  • Simulating the impact of changes in drivers (volumes, discounts, service levels) on product and customer profitability.

PaPM can feed its results back into SAC planning and S/4HANA reporting, turning your twin into a realistic economic model, not just a spreadsheet on steroids.

4. Group Reporting: Real-Time Group Perspective

For group-level FP&A, S/4HANA Group Reporting is critical:

  • It consolidates group financials close to the source, enabling faster closes and near real-time group views.
  • Integrated with SAC, it allows group-level planning and scenario modeling on top of live consolidated data.

This closes the gap between statutory reporting, management reporting, and FP&A scenarios.

5. SAP Signavio: The Process Twin

Finance outcomes are driven by processes: order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, record-to-report, and forecast-to-fulfill. SAP Signavio adds a process-intelligence layer:

  • Process mining shows how these processes really run (cycle times, rework, bottlenecks).
  • You can link these insights to financial impacts—e.g., how process delays affect DSO, inventory, and working capital.

When you overlay Signavio insights onto your financial twin, FP&A can model not just financial numbers but the process changes needed to achieve them.

 

 

 

Putting It Together: An SAP-Based Financial Digital Twin Architecture

At a high level, your architecture might look like this:

  1. Data foundation
    • S/4HANA (and Central Finance if needed) holds harmonized actuals and master data.
    • Group Reporting provides consolidated views.
  2. Modeling and simulation
    • PaPM and CO-PA model profitability and key drivers.
    • Signavio models and analyses process flows.
  3. Planning and analytics
    • SAC hosts planning models, scenarios, dashboards, and predictive analytics.
  4. User experience
    • FP&A, business controllers, and business leaders access insights mainly through SAC and Fiori apps.

Step-by-Step: How to Design Your Financial Digital Twin

This is where many CFOs ask, “Where do we start?” Here’s a pragmatic sequence you can follow.

Step 1: Start with Business Questions, Not Tools

Clarify the decisions you want the twin to support. Examples:

  • “How exposed is our EBITDA and cash to a 10–15% demand shock in our top three markets?”
  • “Which products and customers are truly profitable after cost-to-serve?”
  • “How much working capital can we release without hurting service levels?”

Rank these questions and commit to solving the top three in your first iteration. This ensures your twin delivers visible business value quickly.

Step 2: Harmonize Data and Dimensions in S/4HANA

A digital twin fails if it rests on inconsistent structures. Work with your finance and IT teams to:

  • Standardize chart of accounts, profit centers, cost centers, and key reporting dimensions.
  • Ensure master data (customers, products, materials) is aligned across entities.
  • Confirm that the Universal Journal captures enough detail for your FP&A use cases.

This may sound “plumbing-like,” but it is foundational. Better models cannot fix broken master data.

Step 3: Build Driver Models in PaPM and CO-PA

Once the data foundation is solid, model your business logic:

  • Identify key drivers: volumes, prices, discounts, service levels, productivity, FX rates, etc.
  • Use PaPM (and, where relevant, CO-PA) to define how these drivers flow into revenues, costs, and profitability.
  • Validate the models with business owners using historical data—do they reflect reality well enough?

Aim for models that are simple enough to understand but rich enough to support decision-making.

Step 4: Implement Driver-Based Planning and Scenarios in SAC

Next, bring the twin to life for planners and business partners:

  • Build SAC planning models aligned to S/4HANA structures (no “shadow hierarchies” in Excel).
  • Replace line-item data entry with driver-based input where possible (e.g., volume and price instead of revenue by account; FTE and merit increases instead of salary lines).
  • Configure scenario and version management so FP&A can maintain base, upside, downside, and stress scenarios in parallel.

This is where business users start to “feel” the twin: they change a driver and immediately see financial impacts.

Step 5: Connect Group-Level View with Group Reporting

If you operate as a group:

  • Integrate Group Reporting with SAC so consolidated actuals flow directly into planning models.
  • Use the same structures for management and statutory views where possible to reduce reconciliation.
  • Model group scenarios: acquisitions, divestitures, segment realignments, and their impacts on leverage and covenants.

This helps your board-level discussions move from static decks to interactive, scenario-driven conversations.

Step 6: Add Process Intelligence with SAP Signavio

Once the financial twin is running, enrich it with process insights:

  • Use Signavio to analyze critical processes affecting revenue recognition, cost, and working capital (e.g., billing, collections, procurement, production).
  • Quantify how process changes (faster collections, fewer disputes, better forecast accuracy) move financial metrics.
  • Incorporate these into your PaPM and SAC models as additional levers.

Now your twin does not just answer “What if revenue drops?” but also “What if we improve our collection process by 5 days?”

Step 7: Build a Continuous Learning Loop

A digital twin should get smarter over time:

  • In SAC, set up dashboards comparing actuals vs. plan/forecast at multiple levels (entity, product, customer, segment).
  • Track forecast accuracy and bias by business area and driver.
  • Periodically recalibrate drivers and assumptions in PaPM and SAC models based on observed performance.

Over time, this turns the twin into a trusted decision-support system rather than a one-off project.

 

 

Bringing the Twin to Life: Three FP&A Use Cases

Concrete stories make the concept real for your stakeholders. Here are three you can adapt for your industry.

Use Case 1: Protecting Margins in a Demand Downturn

A global manufacturer sees early signals of softening demand:

  • S/4HANA and SAC show a drop in order intake for specific regions and products.
  • FP&A uses the twin to model a 15% volume decline and its impact on gross margin, plant utilization, and cash over the next two quarters.
  • They test combinations of levers: pricing actions, marketing cuts, production scale-backs, and working-capital measures (inventory reductions, payment-term changes).
  • Management selects a balanced response that protects strategic investments while preserving liquidity, all backed by quantified scenarios.

Instead of reactive across-the-board cuts, the digital twin enables targeted, data-driven action.

Use Case 2: Capacity Expansion and Capex Decisions

A business unit proposes a new production line:

  • PaPM models show how higher capacity and utilization would impact fixed-cost absorption and margins.
  • Group Reporting and SAC project the effect on group-level earnings, leverage, and covenants.
  • Signavio reveals that existing logistics and order-fulfillment processes are already a bottleneck; without fixing them, the new capacity would not be fully utilized.
  • The final decision bundles capex with process improvements, all tested in the twin before committing funds.

This elevates capex approvals from “business case PDFs” to interactive, scenario-based decisions.

Use Case 3: Continuous Working-Capital Steering

Working capital is often a major, but under-optimized, source of cash:

  • The twin brings together DSO, DPO, inventory days, and order patterns from S/4HANA.
  • SAC running predictive models and driver-based forecasts shows future cash conversion under different collection, payment, and inventory strategies.
  • Treasury and FP&A jointly manage liquidity by simulating changes to terms, discount programs, and inventory policies.

Here, the digital twin becomes a daily steering tool, not just a quarterly working-capital report.

How to Talk About This Within Your Organization

For CFOs and FP&A leaders, positioning is key. A few suggestions:

  • With the board:  Emphasize resilience, speed of response, and better capital allocation, not system features.
  • With business leaders: Focus on usable scenarios, transparency on drivers, and the ability to test decisions safely before executing.
  • With IT and SAP teams: Frame it as an architecture and data challenge tied to specific business outcomes, not just another reporting project.

You are not “installing a tool”; you are building a new way for your organization to see itself and make decisions.

Final Thought: FP&A as the Architect of the Financial Digital Twin

The digital twin is a strategic opportunity for FP&A to step up as the architect of performance, not just the reporter of results. SAP gives you the building blocks, but only finance can define the questions, drivers, and scenarios that matter.

If you’re starting this journey, pick one or two high-impact use cases, assemble the right SAP components around them, and prove the value quickly. From there, your financial digital twin can expand step by step—until it becomes the default way your company plans, decides, and acts.

 

Feel free to connect—I'm excited to collaborate and help your team unlock the full potential of financial planning!

entry.title: Accelerate Insights with the new Quick Builder in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Data-driven decisions shouldn’t wait. The new Quick Builder in SAP Analytics Cloud optimized story reimagines how business users and analysts move from data to decisions - faster, guided, and with best practices built in. While exploring a table in an optimized story, Quick Builder provides a simple, guided experience that shortens the path to value.

 

What is the Quick Builder for optimized stories

Quick Builder is a simplified builder panel for the table widget that you can launch in view mode from the table’s action menu or via script. It enables out-of-the-box, lightweight data exploration within stories:

  • Quickly change which dimensions and measures (or accounts) appear in the table.
  • Swap axes and rearrange objects across rows and columns.
  • Add or remove objects without leaving the story.
  • Modify dimension settings such as hierarchy selection and display options.
  • Define settings for rows and columns directly in context.
  • Automatically adapt to each table’s data source. When you navigate to another table on the story page that uses a different data model, Quick Builder updates to show the corresponding objects for that model, keeping exploration fluid without manual reconfiguration.

 

How to launch Quick Builder

  • From the UI: Open Quick Builder from the table’s context menu in view mode for immediate, guided exploration.
  • Via APIs: Use openQuickBuilder() and closeQuickBuilder() for programmatic control, enabling guided or customized analytic flows.

 

How Quick Builder evolves the navigation panel 

The navigation panel was originally introduced in analytic applications and can be opened programmatically using the script APIs. Quick Builder is an advanced evolution of the navigation panel, built on the new table experience. Compared with the navigation panel, it adds:
  • Deselect all
  • The compact display feature, previously available only at runtime, is now also supported during design time for BW Data Models
  • Separate arrangement of totals
  • Enabled by the story designer during design time
  • Launch from the context menu in addition to the existing script-based capability

It also intentionally changes some behaviours to streamline interaction:

  • No “available items” : To keep the interface clean and maximize canvas space, Quick Builder does not provide “available items” area.  All actions, such as adding or removing dimensions and measures, are available directly within Quick Builder.

 

Quick Builder vs. Data Analyzer

Use Quick Builder for lightweight, in‑story data exploration – ideally for fast table configuration that stay in context with your story. Use Data Analyzer for deeper, more comprehensive analysis. Results from Quick Builder apply seamlessly to story interactions such as linked analyses, filters, and other interactive features.

 

2026/QRC1: What to expect

New stories

When you create a new story, the New Table Experience is applied by default. If you enable Quick Builder at design time, it will be available to viewers in these newly created stories.

Existing optimized stories

If your story already contains a table, switch it to the New Table Experience and enable the “Enable Quick Builder” option in the table styling panel. Quick Builder will then be accessible to viewers at runtime through the context menu.
If your story uses the Navigation Panel, the switch to the New Table Experience will seamlessly replace it with Quick Builder—no additional conversion or migration work required.

Converted classic stories and analytic applications

When you convert a classic story or analytic application to an optimized story in QRC1, the New Table Experience is applied automatically. If you enable Quick Builder at design time, it will be available to viewers. If the classic analytic application uses the Navigation Panel, it is seamlessly replaced by Quick Builder—no manual migration needed.

 

2026/QRC2: What’s coming next

We plan to automatically convert existing tables to the New Table Experience during story design time wherever possible, removing the manual step described above. As part of this conversion, the Navigation Panel will be automatically replaced by Quick Builder—again, with no manual migration required.  

 

Getting started with the Quick Bilder in optimized stories:

  • For new stories, make sure Quick Builder is enabled during design time, or use the scripting API to open Quick Builder so that it is available at runtime.
  • For existing optimized stories, switch your tables to the New Table Experience and enable “Enable Quick Builder” in the table styling panel.
  • For classic stories and analytic applications you are converting—whether through manual conversion or mass migration—the resulting optimized stories will automatically use the New Table Experience starting with the QRC1 release. You only need to enable Quick Builder during design time, and it will be available to story viewers at runtime.

 

See it in Action:

 

 

Outlook

This release marks just the beginning of what Quick Builder will offer for light‑weight data exploration within stories. You can expect continuous enhancements - expanding Quick Builder to support additional widgets such as charts, and introducing new capabilities and functions, including filtering and more.

As always, your feedback plays a crucial role in shaping our roadmap. We’re excited to continue evolving together as we reimagine how Quick Builder can empower decision‑making and drive insights across the enterprise.

 

Link to other related blogs

 New Table Builder Panel Becomes the Standard for Story Creation 

Support of a Compact Display for Tables (Live BW)

entry.title: Resolving SAP Analytics Cloud Issues After a RISE Migration
entry.content:
Field Experience and Practical Fixes

As part of a recent RISE upgrade of our SAP S/4HANA system, we encountered several issues in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) that required careful analysis and remediation. This blog captures the key challenges we faced, how we diagnosed them, and the practical solutions that helped us stabilize SAC post-migration.

For context, our SAC tenant is connected to the S/4HANA system using an Import Connection, and CDS views serve as the primary data source for all models and stories.

  • Data Source Schema Changes Leading to Mapping Errors

One of the first issues we observed post-upgrade was a schema mismatch error in SAC, typically displayed as “The schema of the data source has changed. Unable to map ‘XYZ’”. This is a common scenario after system upgrades and usually indicates changes in the underlying data source.

In our case, the RISE upgrade introduced structural changes in the CDS views, which caused existing import jobs in SAC to become incompatible with the updated schema. Although the models and stories were intact, the import jobs could no longer recognize certain fields.

Based on SAP Note 3339716, the resolution was to re-create the import jobs so that SAC could re-read and align itself with the updated CDS structures. Once recreated, the mappings were correctly refreshed and the imports executed successfully.

 

  • Data Queries Returning Empty Result Sets

Another critical issue we faced was SAC import jobs completing successfully but returning empty datasets. Initial checks on the SAC side showed no configuration errors, which pointed us toward the S/4HANA backend.

During a RISE upgrade, hierarchy-related tables are migrated from legacy tables to new ones. Some examples include:

  • HRRP_DIRECTORY → HRRP_DIR_N
  • HRRP_ATTR_NODE → HRRP_ATTR_NODE_N

In our experience, the migration steps responsible for transferring data into these new tables had either not been executed or had failed, leaving the new hierarchy tables empty. As a result, CDS views dependent on these tables returned no data, and SAC imports had nothing to load.

As per SAP Note 3527602, this issue can be resolved by ensuring successful execution of the following migration steps:

  • CL_SDM_HRRP_DIRECTORY
  • CL_SDM_HRRP_DIRECTORYT
  • CL_SDM_HRRP_ND_CONVERT

Alternatively, the HRRP_REP report can be run to manually replicate the hierarchies. Once the hierarchy data was restored, the SAC imports immediately began returning valid results.

  • Data Format Inconsistencies and Unexpected Zero Values After Import

Not all issues manifested as failed import jobs. In some cases, the imports completed successfully, but the data itself was in incorrect format or populated with zero values. 

This proved to be one of the more subtle but impactful issues. Since there were no structural changes in certain CDS views, the import jobs did not fail outright. However, post-migration changes—such as modified calculation logic, altered joins, or updated underlying tables—affected how data was returned.

One of the best examples to explain this is WBS Element which usually has two formats - external format, which may be in the form of WBS01-01-1-1 and internal format which may exist in the form of WBS010111. In our context, prior to migration the fields which were returning the data in the external format, post migration started returning data in internal format, which caused master data discrepancies in the SAC system.

To address this, we performed a detailed validation by:

  • Comparing pre- and post-migration data directly in S/4HANA
  • Reviewing CDS view logic and associations for any functional changes
  • Revalidating measures, transformations, mappings and aggregations within SAC

This step was crucial to ensure reporting accuracy, as such issues can easily go unnoticed until business users start questioning the numbers.

Conclusion: Key Learnings from the RISE Migration

From our experience, a RISE upgrade can have significant downstream impacts on SAP Analytics Cloud, even when SAC itself has not changed. Many of the issues are not outright failures but subtle data and structure inconsistencies that require careful investigation across both SAC and S/4HANA.

Our key takeaways:

  • Always expect CDS view changes after a RISE upgrade and plan to re-create SAC import jobs
  • Validate hierarchy migrations early, especially when analytics depend on hierarchical reporting
  • Do not rely solely on job success status—data validation is just as important as technical execution
  • Close collaboration between Basis, ABAP, and Analytics teams significantly reduces resolution time

Proactively reviewing these areas post-migration can help avoid reporting disruptions and ensure a smooth transition for business users relying on SAC for decision-making.

entry.title: New Table Builder Panel Becomes the Standard for Story Creation
entry.content:
Introduction

This document highlights an important evolution for the SAP Analytics Cloud Story creation experience and the Table widget.

It aims to guide story designers and stakeholders through the transition to the new Table Build Experience and to provide clear visibility into its planned evolution and roadmap.

With this update, we continue to simplify and harmonize the content creation workflow in Stories, ensuring consistency, usability, and long-term innovation readiness.

 

The New Table Build Experience Is The Default For New Stories

Last year, with the 2025 Q3 QRC release, SAP Analytics Cloud introduced the new Table Build Experience for the Optimized Design Mode.

This redesign marked a major step forward in how tables are created, configured, and consumed in SAC.

The new experience offers:

  • A more intuitive and guided workflow for story designers
  • A builder panel aligned with the Chart Builder paradigm
  • A scalable technical foundation enabling innovation
  • Improved usability and consistency across widgets

All new capabilities and innovations introduced with this experience are detailed in the SAP Community article:

The New Table Building Experience in SAP Analytics Cloud

So, what’s new in the 2026 Q1 QRC release?

With the 2026 Q1 QRC release, the New Table Build Experience becomes the default table experience for any new Story created in this release.

This means:

  • Any newly created optimized Story will automatically use the new Table Build Experience
  • No additional activation or opt-in is required

This milestone reflects the maturity and stability of the new framework.

 

The New Table Build Experience Feature Parity

Between the 2025 Q3 QRC and the 2026 Q1 QRC releases, all previously identified gaps and functional restrictions have been addressed.

Our objective was clear: harmonize and extend table capabilities while ensuring full feature parity with the legacy table experience.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC release, we have achieved full functional parity for optimized stories.

This ensures that:

  • Existing analytical scenarios are fully supported
  • Planning-enabled tables maintain their capabilities
  • Advanced formatting and structural options are available
  • No regression is introduced for enterprise use cases

This milestone confirms the readiness of the new Table Build Experience as the standard going forward.

 

What Happens To Your Existing Optimized Stories

2026 Q1 QRC

If you have existing optimized Stories containing tables, there is no automatic change in this release.

Moving to the New Table Build Experience remains an “opt-in” manual action at Design Time.

You still have time to:

  • Evaluate the new experience
  • Test it in your development or test tenants
  • Validate your specific business scenarios
  • Prepare your organization for the transition

2026 Q2 QRC : be prepared !

Starting with the planned 2026 Q2 QRC release:

  • The New Table Build Experience will be enforced by default for all Stories.
  • The switch will occur automatically when you edit a Story.
  • At view time, the new Table is already enforced for almost all tables in optimised Stories (with a few remaining exceptions).

Further communication and detailed information will be shared ahead of this important change.

We strongly recommend that customers proactively validate their critical Stories ahead of this release to ensure a smooth transition.

 

Share Your Feedback

We want this transition to the New Table Build Experience to be as smooth and valuable as possible.

Your feedback directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please share your thoughts and experience through:

  • SAP Community discussions
  • Customer Influence channels

Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Support of a Compact Display for Tables (Live BW)
entry.content:
Introduction

As part of our continuous innovation for Live BW connectivity, we are introducing Compact Display for Tables with the 2026 Q1 QRC release.

Compact Display is designed to simplify complex table structures by consolidating multiple dimensions into a single, structured hierarchy within a row or column.

The goal is to enhance readability, optimize space usage, and improve analytical clarity, especially in enterprise scenarios with deep dimensional breakdowns.

For customers familiar with the “Universal Display Hierarchy” in SAP BEx Query Designer, Compact Display delivers a similar conceptual benefit in SAC’s Optimized Experience, while being purpose-built for SAC architecture and modern UX standards.

 

What is the Feature “Compact Display”?

Compact Display allows you to group multiple dimensions on a row or column axis into a single structured display, enabling expandable nodes instead of separate stacked dimensions.

What it does

When activated on an axis:

  • All dimensions bound to that axis are grouped into one single row or column structure
  • The structure follows the top-to-bottom order of entities bound to the axis
  • Users can expand or collapse nodes interactively
  • The header displays the names of the compacted Dimensions/Measures
  • Display Attributes are not compacted and remain as separate rows/columns

What it does not do

  • If the axis contains only measures, Compact Display:
    • Is unchecked
    • Is disabled
    • Displays the tooltip:
      "You can't apply compact display because this axis lacks dimensions or contains only measures."
  • If no dimensions or measures are bound to the axis, the option is disabled.
  • Compact Display does nothing when only measures exist on the axis.

Why it matters

In real-life enterprise reporting scenarios, tables can quickly become:

  • Wide and hard to read
  • Vertically heavy
  • Difficult to navigate

Compact Display helps you to:

  • Improve density without losing structure
  • Enhance hierarchical readability
  • Provide a cleaner user experience

 

How to Use It

Compact Display is available in the Optimized Experience. If not yet enabled, you must opt-in to the “New Table Builder Experience”.

Step-by-Step Activation

  1. Open your story
  2. Select your Table
  3. In the Builder Panel, locate the Row or Column axis
  4. Click the “…” menu
  5. Select Compact Display

Behavior logic

Scenario

Compact Display Option

No real dimension on axis (only measures or empty)

Disabled, unchecked, tooltip shown

At least one dimension added

Enabled; any new dimension or measure added to the axis will be added to the Compact Display

Last real dimension removed

Disabled again, unchecked, tooltip shown

 

See It in Action

 

Share Your Feedback

Compact Display is an enhancement for Live BW scenarios, and your feedback is essential.

It directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please share your thoughts and experience through:

  • SAP Community discussions
  • Customer Influence channels

Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC): A Beginner's Guide - Part 3
entry.content:
Introduction

This is an SAP blog series on SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). This is the third part of the blog series SAP Business Data Cloud - A Beginner's Guide. If you missed the first and second part please go through that and let me know your feedback or comments. 

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC): A Beginner's Guide - Part 1 - In the first part, I covered Introduction to SAP Business Data Cloud, Why do we need SAP Business Data Cloud, and Key Components of SAP Business Data Cloud. 

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC): A Beginner's Guide - Part 2 - In the second part, I covered the Architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud. 

I hope you have got a basic understanding about SAP Business Data Cloud and Architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud .

In this part, I'm planning to cover what are the components of SAP Business Data Cloud and it's role in SAP BDC in beginner's language. 

SAP Datasphere

Let’s start with SAP Datasphere, which is the core data modeling and integration component of SAP Business Data Cloud.
Datasphere integrates and harmonizes data from SAP and non-SAP sources, and helps in ensuring raw data is structured and meaningful. Raw data alone isn’t sufficient for analytics or AI it needs context and semantic definitions.

SAP Datasphere offers options for semantic enrichment like classify datasets as facts, dimensions, hierarchies, or text, and specify field meanings such as date, currency, or unit, and also setting aggregation rules like sum, average, min, or max. and it converts raw data into business-ready, semantically enriched models.

Datasphere supports prebuilt models from SAP as well as custom models combining SAP-managed data with partner or customer data products. These models can be consumed by SAP Analytics Cloud or AI/ML workflows in Databricks.
In SAP BDC, Datasphere acts as the trusted foundation, bridging raw data from source systems to analytics and AI, ensuring consistency and semantic clarity across the platform.

SAP Databricks

Next, let’s discuss SAP Databricks, the AI and machine learning engine within BDC.
Databricks provides a collaborative platform for data scientists to develop and deploy AI/ML models on top of harmonized BDC data. It covers the entire ML lifecycle, from experimentation to production, including advanced cases like generative AI.

Data engineers can perform ingestion, transformation, and monitoring while Delta Share enables seamless, zero-copy access to BDC data products. This ensures AI/ML workflows operate on live, governed data without duplication.
In BDC, role of Databricks is to extend harmonized data products with intelligence, delivering predictive and actionable insights that can be visualized in Analytics Cloud or reused in other BDC components.

SAP BW

Now let’s look at SAP BW integration. Many organizations already have valuable historical data in SAP BW or BW/4HANA, and BDC allows you to leverage this data in the cloud without disruption.
The integration follows three steps:

  1. Lift : to move your on-premise BW system to the BDC private cloud for extended maintenance and reduced infrastructure management.
  2. Shift : to generate data products from BW Info Providers so they can be consumed in Datasphere, Analytics Cloud, or Databricks.
  3. Innovate : to adopt SAP-managed data products and Intelligent Applications, and modernizing analytics workflows over time.

In BDC, BW provides historical, trusted enterprise data as ready-to-use data products, enabling AI/ML, dashboards, and custom analytics scenarios while preserving previous investments.

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)

Next component is SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). SAC is the front-end of BDC.

It provides dashboards, charts, tables, maps, and stories for interactive exploration of harmonized and enriched data. Users can drill down, filter, and perform planning functions

Analytics Cloud integrates with Datasphere, Databricks, and BW models, so all visualizations are based on trusted, governed data. It also leverages AI/ML features like predictive insights and the ‘Just Ask’ natural language query assistant.
Analytics Cloud supports both prebuilt Intelligent Applications and custom dashboards, enabling users to turn enriched data and AI insights into actionable decisions.

Its role in BDC is to provide a unified interface for business users, helping them analyze, plan, and act on data efficiently.

Data Foundation Services

Finally, let’s discuss the Foundation Services of SAP Business Data Cloud.
Foundation Services manage SAP-managed data products: they replicate, harmonize, and store them in the SAP HANA Cloud Data Lake. The data is stored in highly efficient files rather than tables, enabling large-scale, high-performance read operations.
Foundation Services also support Delta Share, an open protocol that allows data to be shared securely with zero copying. Consumers such as SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks, or other analytics tools can access data efficiently while maintaining governance.
Discovery of available data products is facilitated via Open Resource Discovery (ORD), which exposes technical definitions, metadata, and business descriptions. Users can explore products before generating or installing them.
In BDC, Foundation Services act as the backbone, ensuring that all SAP-managed data products are up-to-date, discoverable, and securely available for analytics, AI/ML, and Intelligent Applications. Without Foundation Services, data products and intelligent applications would not have a trusted, governed environment for reliable consumption.

 

Conclusion

In this blog we covered the components of SAP Business Data Cloud and it's role in SAP BDC. This is the part 3 of the SAP Business Data Cloud - A beginner's guide series.

In case if you missed the part 1 and 2, here is the links:

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC): A Beginner's Guide - Part 1 

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC): A Beginner's Guide - Part 2

If you have any feedback, questions or queries, please post them as comments. I will try to answer the maximum as per my knowledge.

entry.title: Blend Data in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP Analytics Cloud is a self-service solution, which means that story designers regularly face the task of displaying data from various sources (datasets or models) in a single visualization. In this blog, I would like to show how this can be implemented. The reason I wrote this blog is that blending for tables was gone in optimized stories. Since it is back now with the new table experience I would like present it to you as might not have heard of it, as Blending was removed from SACS21 then. If you don’t want to read the whole blog you can register for the one our Live Session Exploring Data Blending in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories Exploring Data Blending in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories

The data presented in this blog is based on HANA live data sources, which are available in the Learning Hub's practice system, so everything demonstrated here can be tried out by yourself. Let’s start with it.

In reporting, data from different data sources is regularly displayed side by side so that the key figures from these data sources are visible at a glance. The data from these sources is often related to each other, as they use the same dimensions.

In both tables shown here, the same dimension Region is displayed. It makes sense to present this data in a single table so that the two key figures # of Orders and Sales Revenue are nicely placed next to each other and can be easily compared. Of course it would be dangerous to just make the two tables look like one table, because if data is changing only in only place and not in the other you might end up comparing the wrong figures. To get it straight we really need to tell SAC that a dimension in one model based on same or almost same members in the other model.

This is done via the feature "Link Dimensions." Here, the dimension that is available in both data sources needs to be linked.

It may also be desirable to create a calculation whose formula uses key figures from both data sources. In the following table, both dimensions are linkrf and a calculation has taken place. The column Sales Revenue per Order is exactly such a calculation. Another scenario would be to add an attribute which available only in one data source, but not in the other.

Basic Procedure

In order to display the data from both models in a single table, two steps must be carried out:

  1. Linking the two models via one or more common dimensions
  2. Adding a linked model in the table or chart that is supposed to display the data from both models.

To 1. Linking the two models via a common dimension

  1. In the story's edit mode, the linking is done in the data area using the button
  2. In the Link Dimensions window, you select the dimensions that have matching values. In my simple example, it is sufficient to set the dimension Region for both models, i.e., on the left and right, and confirm the settings with the Set button.




However, there may also be cases where two or more dimensions need to be linked, for example, if year and month are separate dimensions and the data should be displayed at the month level.

To 2. Adding a linked model in the table or chart that is supposed to display the data from both models.

If you now add a table or a chart in the story, you can now assign a linked model in the builder section of the designer, i.e., where the table structure is defined, for example:

Here I now select my linked model PACIFICA_SHIPPING_INFO:

Once the model is assigned, objects from both models can be included in the table structure. Here, for example, as seen in the row area:

The icon in front of PACIFICA_ORDER_FINANCE indicates that Region is a linked demension and the dot icon after Region indicates that Region is coming from the primary model.

The table with Sales Revenue from PACIFICA_ORDER_FINANCE and # of Orders from the model PACIFICA_SHIPPING_INFO now looks like this:

Finding the right (collection of) dimension(s)

It can happen that the data from two models cannot be properly combined, because the wrong dimension was linked. If there is a problem with the correctness of the displayed data for a dimension the visualization would not be rendered at all and therefore would look like this:

As you can see there is an indicator for the problem (!). If you click on it you will see this:

You will need to put Store dimension the chart.

In this case it is not sufficient to link on Region and therefore must be done at a more granular level, which is to link the Store dimension. Since here a Store is always assigned to exactly one Region, the link on Region can be removed without causing any limitations or incorrect values.

As soon as Store in the left model is linked with Store in the right model, this message disappears and instead another message appears. This time, it is the first of the two messages, which gives me a hint that the linking via Store is still not correct.

Sometimes visualization cannot be rendered, as there is a lack of uniqueness in the values. In those cases, where for example you have several stores with the same name, uniqueness is achieved by linking not via the description (default setting), but via the ID:

When linking dimension please consider:

  • In the example presented, the data of two models were linked together, but it is also possible to link three or more models. However, this increases complexity, and it is naturally worth thinking about whether to integration of the data on the model level which means before creating the story. If would do so you would get a better reusability and a better performance in most cases.
  • Two HANA Live data sources were linked together. Not all Live data sources can be linked, and here I refer to the help.sap.com. There are further restrictions related to the blending functionality, including displaying totals, Top N, sorting, creating variances based on combined calculations.
